desc: Cold-water start with chicken bones, slow-skim, no-boil 4-hour simmer with mirepoix and bay. Clear, deeply gelatinous, neutral enough to use across French sauce work, Italian risotti, Chinese soups, and Mexican caldos.

ingredients.item: raw chicken bones (carcasses, backs, wings, necks)

title: Furikake (homemade rice seasoning)
desc: Spent kombu and katsuobushi from a fresh batch of dashi, dried in a low oven and chopped into a rice-grain-sized seasoning, then mixed with toasted sesame, shredded nori, and salt. The end-of-the-batch move that turns dashi byproducts into a pantry staple.

ingredients.item: spent kombu (from a fresh batch of dashi)
spent katsuobushi (from a fresh batch of dashi)

title: Grilled whole squid with nuoc cham (mực nướng)
desc: Whole cleaned squid (tubes and tentacles intact) marinated briefly in fish sauce, garlic, and lemongrass, grilled over high heat for 90 seconds per side until charred at the edges and just opaque through, served with classic Vietnamese nuoc cham — lime, fish sauce, sugar, garlic, chili. The 90-second high-heat window over fire instead of oil.

ingredients.item: whole cleaned squid (small to medium, 8 to 12 cm tubes, with tentacles)
lemongrass, finely minced (white tender part only)
fresh red chili (Thai bird), finely minced

equipment: charcoal grill (preferred), gas grill, or grill pan

title: Individual decadent chocolate cakes
desc: Two 4-inch chocolate cakes built on a chocolate-butter emulsion and bloomed Dutch cocoa, with brown sugar for moisture and a turbinado crackle top. No frosting: the cake itself is the payload.

title: Individual fluffy chocolate cakes
desc: Two 4-inch tall, light-crumbed chocolate cakes built on creamed butter, bloomed Dutch cocoa, and sour cream for tenderness. Designed to be served warm with a scoop of ice cream melting into the open crumb.

title: Rice kheer (chawal ki kheer)
desc: Whole milk reduced by 60% with basmati rice, sweetened with sugar, perfumed with green cardamom and saffron, finished with chopped pistachios and almonds. The structural North Indian milk-rice pudding, eaten warm or cold, served at every festival and Sunday meal. Reduction is the technique; patience is the only difficulty.

ingredients.item: granulated sugar (or jaggery for traditional sweetness)

title: Lotus root and herb small bites
desc: Thin lotus root rounds blanched briefly to set the wagon-wheel structure, dressed with herb-oil, and topped per portion with one of: grated cured egg yolk, chili crisp and scallion, sesame paste and black vinegar, or soft soy-cured roe. The format is small-plate appetizer.

ingredients.item: fresh cilantro leaves and tender stems, finely chopped

title: Mala dry-pot fish (gan guo yu)
desc: Sichuan dry-pot. White fish fillet seared, then tossed in red oil rendered from doubanjiang and dried chilies, with lotus root, cauliflower, wood ear, firm tofu, tofu skin, bell pepper, scallion, and cilantro. No broth, no sauce: the rendered oil is the medium.

ingredients.item: firm white fish fillet (cod, halibut, sea bass, or tilapia)
lotus root, peeled and sliced 4 mm thick
firm tofu, pressed and cut into 2 cm cubes
dried tofu skin sticks (fu zhu), rehydrated
red bell pepper, cut into 2 cm pieces
scallions, white parts cut into 4 cm lengths, green parts reserved
neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)
dried Chinese chilies (mix erjingtiao + chao tian jiao)
fresh cilantro leaves and tender stems, rough-chopped

title: Poured-set mapo tofu (douhua mapo)
desc: Hot soy milk whisked with GDL and poured directly into the serving bowl, allowed to set into a freshly-made silken curd, then topped with a thick mapo sauce ladled over at near-boiling. The curd is softer than any commercial silken tofu, the eating experience is custard-soft cradle for the mala sauce above, and the dish hits the table at the highest possible temperature contrast.

ingredients.item: plain unsweetened soy milk (no calcium fortification)
Sichuan peppercorns, freshly toasted and ground

title: Millionaire shortbread
desc: Three-layer Scottish bar: short, salt-forward shortbread base, a thick caramel built from sweetened condensed milk cooked to a deep amber, capped by tempered dark chocolate. Each layer earns its texture from a specific cooking endpoint.

ingredients.item: golden syrup or light corn syrup

title: Miso soup (miso-shiru)
desc: Niban dashi (or fresh ichiban for foreground applications) brought to 80°C, miso whisked in off-heat through a small sieve to break up clumps, finished with rehydrated wakame, cubed silken tofu, and sliced scallion. The miso enters at the end and never sees a boil ,  that single rule separates good miso soup from flat miso soup.

ingredients.item: awase miso (or 50/50 mix white and red)

title: Korean spicy stir-fried squid (ojingeo bokkeum, 오징어볶음)
desc: Cross-hatch-scored squid stir-fried at high heat with onion, carrot, scallion, and a gochujang-and-gochugaru sauce sweetened with mirin and balanced with soy and sesame oil. The Korean expression of the 90-second high-heat squid window. Served with steamed rice or wrapped in lettuce-and-perilla.

ingredients.item: whole cleaned squid (small to medium tubes, with tentacles)
soy sauce (Korean ganjang or Japanese koikuchi)
yellow onion, sliced into 1 cm wedges
green cabbage, cut into 4 cm squares

title: Olive Garden breadsticks (the copycat)
desc: Lightly enriched yeast dough with bread flour, butter, milk, and a small dose of sugar, shaped into 18 cm batons, proofed to pillow-soft, baked, then brushed with garlic butter and dusted with seasoned salt. The chain-restaurant breadstick reverse-engineered to the kitchen-correct version.

ingredients.item: unsalted butter, melted and cooled to lukewarm
garlic, pressed or finely grated on a microplane

title: Onigiri (rice triangles)
desc: Freshly-cooked Japanese short-grain rice formed warm into triangles or rounds with cold-water-and-salt hands, optionally filled with a savory center, and wrapped in a sheet of toasted nori at the moment of eating. Portable, room-temperature, and the canonical Japanese hand-food.

ingredients.item: tuna mayo (canned tuna, drained, mixed with Kewpie)

title: Pav bhaji (Mumbai street vegetable mash)
desc: Boiled and mashed potatoes, peas, cauliflower, and bell peppers cooked in a deeply tomato-spiced ghee-and-onion masala built on pav bhaji masala (a specific commercial blend) and Kashmiri chili. Served with butter-toasted soft pav rolls, raw onion, lemon, and a generous pat of butter melting on top. The defining Mumbai street food.

ingredients.item: russet or yukon gold potatoes, peeled and chunked
green chilies (Thai bird or jalapeño), finely minced
pav bhaji masala (commercial blend, MDH or Everest)
tomato paste (concentrated, for color and depth)
pav rolls (soft Mumbai-style buns) or brioche dinner rolls
red onion, finely diced (for the side)

equipment: wide skillet or griddle for pav

title: Pickled mustard greens (suan cai, 酸菜)
desc: Whole heads of mustard greens lacto-fermented in a 3% salt brine for seven to ten days into a sour, crunchy, deeply funky pickle. The Cantonese and Sichuan tables both reach for it.

ingredients.item: Chinese mustard greens (gai choy / xue li hong / suan cai cabbage)

title: Pork bone broth (zhu gu tang)
desc: Pork bones blanched, then long-simmered for a clear, gelatin-rich Chinese-style broth. The base of Cantonese soups, congee, dumpling fillings, and noodle bowls. Modifications cover the rolling-boil milky version (tonkotsu-style for ramen) and the herbal Cantonese long-soup tradition.

ingredients.item: pork bones (neck, ribs, leg, knuckle, mix is best)

title: Pork floss (rousong, 肉松)
desc: Lean pork shoulder simmered tender with aromatics, shredded fine, then dry-fried in a wok with sugar and soy until the strands separate into a fluffy, dry, faintly sweet topping.

ingredients.item: pork shoulder, lean only (no skin, minimal fat)

title: Raita (cucumber-mint, the household standard)
desc: Plain whole-milk yogurt whisked smooth with grated cucumber, fresh mint, toasted cumin, and a small pinch of black salt or kala namak. The cooling counterpoint to spicy biryani, pulao, and curry. Five minutes of prep; structural to a South Asian meal in a way that "side salad" is not.

ingredients.item: plain whole-milk yogurt (Indian dahi or American whole-milk plain)
green chili (Thai bird, serrano, or jalapeño), finely minced
kala namak (Indian black salt), or fine sea salt

title: Ras malai (chhena dumplings in saffron-cardamom milk)
desc: Fresh chhena (acid-set whole-milk curds, drained and kneaded) shaped into small discs, gently simmered in light sugar syrup until they puff and float, then transferred to reduced milk perfumed with cardamom, saffron, and pistachio. Bengali in origin, North Indian and Pakistani by adoption, served chilled at every wedding and festival on the subcontinent.

ingredients.item: fresh lemon juice (or 30 ml white vinegar)

equipment: wide saucepan or shallow Dutch oven for syrup

title: Roasted pear and gorgonzola tartine
desc: Bosc pear roasted in butter and balsamic until caramelized, smeared over a toasted sourdough slice with gorgonzola dolce, scattered with toasted walnuts, finished with thyme, balsamic, and cracked pepper. Open-faced lunch or light dinner.

ingredients.item: Bosc pear, just-ripe (gives slightly to firm thumb pressure)

title: Salt-and-pepper squid (Cantonese jiao yan you yu, 椒盐鱿鱼)
desc: Cross-hatch-scored squid tubes dredged in a cornstarch-and-rice-flour mix, deep-fried 90 seconds at 175°C, tossed with toasted-and-ground white-pepper salt and crisped chili-garlic-scallion. The 90-second high-heat cooking window for squid, executed at deep-fry intensity. Pinecone curls from the scoring, salt-pepper crust on every cut edge.

ingredients.item: whole or cleaned squid (small to medium tubes, 8 to 12 cm)
neutral oil (peanut, rice bran, or canola, any high-smoke-point neutral oil)
fresh red chili (Thai bird or fresno), finely sliced

title: Salted duck egg yolks (home brine cure)
desc: Raw duck eggs cured thirty-five days in saturated brine, then steamed and the yolks separated. Dense, fudgy, oil-bleeding red-orange yolks for congee, mooncakes, and salted-egg-yolk sauce.

title: Shrimp pulao (jhinga pulao)
desc: Coastal-Indian basmati pilaf cooked with marinated shrimp, ginger-garlic, whole spices, and a coconut-milk-and-stock cooking liquid. Less elaborate than biryani (no parboil-and-layer), more aromatic than plain rice. The shrimp-and-spice flavors infuse into each grain through absorption-method cooking.

ingredients.item: large shrimp (16/20 count), peeled and deveined, tails on or off
ginger-garlic paste (homemade from 15 g each ginger + garlic blended)
green chilies (Thai bird or serrano), slit lengthwise

equipment: heavy 4-quart Dutch oven or wide pot with tight lid

title: Sichuan fish-fragrant eggplant (yu xiang qie zi)
desc: Sichuan classic. Eggplant batons dry-fried until structurally collapsed, then tossed in a sweet-sour-spicy "fish-fragrant" sauce built on doubanjiang, ginger, garlic, scallions, and Chinkiang vinegar. Contains no fish; the name describes the flavor profile traditionally used for fish.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)

title: Sichuan mapo tofu (ma po dou fu)
desc: The Chen-family Chengdu original: silken tofu cubes blanched and held in a doubanjiang-and-douchi red oil sauce thickened with three pours of cornstarch slurry, finished with freshly toasted Sichuan peppercorn powder. The seven-flavor profile (numbing, spicy, hot, fragrant, savory, fresh, tender) is the codified Sichuan target.

ingredients.item: Sichuan peppercorns, freshly toasted and ground

title: Soft-boiled congee egg
desc: A 6½-minute soft-boiled egg cracked into the hot bowl just before eating: white set, yolk still molten, the heat of the porridge finishes the cook and binds yolk into broth.

ingredients.item: water (enough to cover the eggs by 2 cm)

title: Soy-glazed peanuts
desc: Raw peanuts toasted in oil until they smell roasted, then tossed off-heat with a soy-sugar-vinegar glaze that lacquers as it cools. Crunchy, savory, faintly sweet, perfect for a small dish on the congee table.

ingredients.item: Sichuan peppercorns, lightly toasted and crushed

title: Spaghetti bolognese (ragù alla bolognese, the household version)
desc: Long-cooked Emilia-Romagna meat ragù: pancetta rendered into the soffritto, ground beef and pork browned hard for Maillard depth, white wine deglaze, milk to tenderize the meat proteins, and a small dose of tomato to round the dish without dominating it. Served over spaghetti as the household-American convention; tagliatelle is the canonical Italian pairing.

ingredients.item: dry white wine (Pinot Grigio, Trebbiano, or Verdicchio)
canned San Marzano tomatoes, hand-crushed (with their juice)
dried spaghetti (or tagliatelle, the canonical pairing)
parmigiano reggiano, finely grated, for finishing

title: Squid ink pasta with calamari (linguine al nero di seppia)
desc: Squid simmered 35 minutes with white wine, San Marzano tomatoes, garlic, and chili flakes until the collagen hydrolyzes from rubber to silk, finished with concentrated squid ink for the dramatic black coloration and a faint marine umami. Tossed with linguine and emulsified with starchy pasta water. The 30-minute low-simmer window for squid, in Italian.

ingredients.item: whole cleaned squid (medium tubes, 12 to 18 cm)
dry white wine (verdicchio, soave, or pinot grigio)
San Marzano tomatoes (whole, peeled, in juice)
squid ink (from packets, jarred, or reserved fresh ink sacs)

title: Stuffed lotus root with pork
desc: Lotus root rings with seasoned ground pork pressed into the air channels, pan-seared to set, then braised in soy-aromatic broth until the lotus softens and the pork has bound the rings together. The Cantonese braised version; Hunan deep-fries the same construction.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)
scallion whites, cut into 4 cm lengths
cornstarch, mixed with 30 ml / 2 tbsp water

title: Tandoori chicken salad sandwich (Hunter's version)
desc: Poached chicken thigh shredded fine, bound with a tandoori-masala-spiced mayo, layered with crisp celery, grated carrot, and toasted walnuts, and served on a buttered toasted croissant. The American chicken-salad-sandwich form re-engineered through a Punjabi spice base; the tandoori-mayo is the structural pivot that makes the dish.

ingredients.item: tandoori masala (Shan, MDH, or homemade blend)
cayenne or Kashmiri chili powder (for added heat)
carrot, finely grated on the box grater
butter lettuce or red-leaf lettuce, washed and dried

title: Ukoy
desc: Filipino shrimp and vegetable fritters: a thin rice-flour batter wrapped around mung bean sprouts, sweet potato, and carrot matchsticks with a single shrimp pressed into each disc, deep-fried to a glassy shatter and served with a sharp garlic-chili-cane-vinegar dip.

ingredients.item: large shrimp, peeled and deveined, tails removed

title: Vegetable broth
desc: Kombu, dried shiitake, onion, carrot, celery, leek, and a tomato simmered briefly into a clean, deeply umami-rich vegetable broth. The east-meets-west default, with modifications for purely Western mirepoix and Japanese kombu-shiitake dashi.

ingredients.item: leek, white and pale green parts only

title: Vegetarian mapo tofu (sù má pó dòu fu)
desc: Mapo tofu rebuilt around mushroom umami: fresh shiitake browned to mushroom duxelles, dried shiitake stock as the cooking liquid, and the rest of the doubanjiang-douchi-Sichuan-peppercorn architecture intact. The mushroom granules sub for the beef granules with comparable textural and savory weight.

ingredients.item: Sichuan peppercorns, freshly toasted and ground

title: Yaki udon (stir-fried udon noodles)
desc: Thick, chewy udon noodles stir-fried over high heat with sliced pork belly, cabbage, carrot, scallion, and a soy-mirin-oyster-sauce glaze that reduces and clings to the noodles. The Kitakyushu-origin lighter, soy-based cousin to yakisoba ,  faster, deeper, and the canonical Japanese weeknight noodle stir-fry.

ingredients.item: pork belly, sliced 3 mm thick across the grain
green cabbage, cut into 4 cm squares

title: Chocolate-butter emulsion
desc: Melt chocolate and butter together into a single glossy mass before any egg, sugar, or flour. The cocoa butter and dairy fat fuse into one stable fat phase, which becomes the structural and flavor backbone of the bake.

title: Dashi (kombu and katsuobushi extraction)
desc: Cold-soak or warm kombu to 60 to 65°C / 140 to 150°F to extract glutamate without slime, pull the kelp before any boil, then off-heat steep katsuobushi for 30 to 60 seconds and strain immediately. The umami-on-umami stack (free glutamate from kombu plus inosinate from bonito) that anchors washoku.

title: Dry-frying eggplant (gan bian)
desc: Cook cut eggplant over high heat in a wok with little or no oil until the surface dehydrates and browns and the cell structure collapses inward, then add oil and aromatics. Solves the eggplant-sponge-soaks-oil problem from the heat-and-time direction.

title: Enriched dough
desc: Yeast-raised dough fortified with fat, dairy, eggs, and sugar, softer crumb, richer flavor, and a structural rise that depends on managing the fat's gluten-weakening effect.

title: Hot-set silken tofu (pour-and-set)
desc: Whisk glucono delta-lactone (GDL) into warm soy milk at 70 to 90°C / 160 to 195°F, pour directly into the serving bowl, leave undisturbed 15 to 25 minutes. The slow acidulation sets the soy proteins into a custard-soft curd that wobbles in place. The technique behind Chinese douhua, the poured-set mapo tofu variant, and Japanese silken-tofu desserts.

title: Red oil rendering
desc: Bloom doubanjiang (Pixian chili bean paste) and dried chilies in oil at 140 to 160°C / 285 to 320°F until the oil turns brilliant red and smells distinctly of toasted chili-bean paste. The structural starting move of Sichuan dry-pot, mapo tofu, twice-cooked pork, and most of the mala canon.

title: Salting eggplant
desc: Cover cut eggplant with salt at 1 to 1.5% by weight, let stand 30 to 45 minutes, blot dry. Pulls water out of the cell network so the eggplant fries with 30 to 40% less oil absorption. Optional and cultivar-dependent.

title: Scoring squid (cross-hatch)
desc: Cut a shallow cross-hatch grid into the inside of a squid tube before slicing. The cuts increase surface area for char and dredge adhesion, force the tube to curl into pinecone shapes when it hits high heat, and prevent the tough collagen sheets from contracting into rubber strips.

title: Century egg (皮蛋, pidan)
desc: Duck (or chicken, quail) egg preserved by alkaline cure for weeks to months. Black-amber translucent white, dark green creamy yolk, sulfurous-savory aroma. The cure does the cooking.

title: Jasmine rice
desc: Long-grain aromatic rice (Oryza sativa indica) from Thailand and adjacent regions. Floral, soft, slightly clingy, around 18% amylose.

title: Salted duck egg (咸鸭蛋, xian ya dan)
desc: Raw duck egg cured in salt brine for thirty to forty days. White stays mostly liquid and saline, yolk turns dense, deep orange-red, oil-bleeding, fudgy. A different ingredient from a century egg.

title: Cantonese white congee
desc: A neutral base of rice cooked at a tremor for an hour into a creamy, near-cream consistency. The rice pretreatment is the technique: oil-and-salt massage, or freeze-then-simmer, breaks the grain so it surrenders its starch fully.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)
scallions, sliced thin on the bias

title: Chicken and ginger congee
desc: The healing-food version. Bone-in chicken thighs simmered into the rice, a heavy ginger load to clean the palate, finished with sesame oil, scallion, and chili oil at the table. The Cantonese kitchen calls this comfort food when someone is sick.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)
rice vinegar, for the table acid

title: Crispy fried shallots (and the shallot oil)
desc: Thinly sliced shallots fried slow in neutral oil until deep gold and shatteringly crisp. The oil that comes free is the bonus ingredient and arguably more useful than the shallots themselves.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (peanut, rice bran, or canola)

title: Cured egg yolk (salt-sugar dry cure)
desc: Raw yolks buried in a 70:30 salt-sugar bed for a week, rinsed, then dried in a low oven or dehydrator until they grate like a hard cheese. Translucent, deep amber, dense umami concentrate.

ingredients.item: fresh large eggs (the freshest you can buy)

title: Ginger-scallion oil (薑蔥油, geung chung yau)
desc: Cantonese aromatic finishing oil. Minced ginger and scallion shocked with smoking-hot neutral oil into a paste that perfumes plain rice, congee, poached chicken, steamed fish, and noodles.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (peanut, rice bran, or canola)

title: Pork and century egg congee (皮蛋瘦肉粥)
desc: The canonical Cantonese savory congee. Bone-in pork shoulder simmered into the rice provides its own stock; cured pidan goes in twice, half during the simmer to bloom into the broth and half at the bowl for the textural contrast.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)
bone-in pork shoulder steak or country-style ribs
scallions, sliced thin on the bias
rice vinegar, for the table acid

title: Basmati rice
desc: Long-grain aromatic rice from the Indian subcontinent, low in amylopectin and high in 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline. Grains stay separate, lengthen dramatically on cooking, and carry a popcorn-pandan aroma that defines the cuisine it anchors.

title: Jameed
desc: Fermented sheep or goat yogurt, salted and sun-dried into stone-hard cones. Reconstitutes into a sour, funky, glutamate-heavy sauce base that no live yogurt approximates.

title: Lamb shoulder (bone-in)
desc: Collagen-rich, fat-marbled forequarter cut from young sheep. Built for slow-braise temperatures where the connective tissue hydrolyzes into gelatin and the meat falls off the bone.

title: Samna (clarified butter)
desc: Butter cooked past its water phase and strained free of milk solids — pure butterfat, around 99% lipid, with the toasted-solid aroma already baked in. The Levantine and North African analog of Indian ghee.

title: Mansaf
desc: Bone-in lamb shoulder simmered for hours, then bathed in a sauce built from reconstituted jameed (fermented dried sheep yogurt). Served over saffron-tinted basmati rice on a base layer of paper-thin shrak bread, crowned with samna-toasted pine nuts and almonds. The Bedouin and Jordanian feast dish, technique-faithful.

ingredients.item: bone-in lamb shoulder, cut into 6 large pieces by the butcher
cold water for the cornstarch slurry
shrak or markook bread (paper-thin Bedouin flatbread)

title: Cooked yogurt sauce
desc: Stabilize yogurt with starch or egg, temper into the hot dish, and hold at a tremor below the boil. Casein curdles above 85 °C without help; the help is what makes a yogurt-based stew possible.

title: Cross-Contamination and the Danger Zone
desc: The 2-hour and 4-hour rules for food in the 40-140°F danger zone, board and tool separation, marinade reuse, leftover cooling, and reheat targets. The orthogonal axis to internal-temperature targets.

title: Egg Doneness and Salmonella
desc: Shell-egg pathogen risk math, when raw or partly cooked yolks are reasonable, and when to reach for pasteurized eggs. Covers carbonara, mayo, hollandaise, mousse, custards, and meringue.

title: Meat Doneness and Pasteurization
desc: USDA published-safe internal temperatures for poultry, pork, beef, and lamb, plus the full time-temperature pasteurization table that explains why thin-sliced and sous-vide approaches can sit below the instantaneous number and still clear safety.

title: Seafood Doneness, Parasites, and Live Handling
desc: Internal-temperature targets for fin fish and shellfish, parasite-freezing rules for raw applications, bivalve live-handling, scombroid and ciguatera basics, and what "sushi-grade" actually means.

title: Velveting
desc: Toss thin-sliced lean protein with salt, baking soda, and a cornstarch slurry, rest 20-30 minutes, then cook briefly in a hot wet medium. The pH shift and gel jacket keep the muscle tender at temperatures that would otherwise wreck it.

title: Thai kitchen
desc: Build the paste from scratch, then cook with it. The aromatics are the same across both recipes — making the paste first makes the curry legible.

title: Classic pancakes
desc: Whole milk soured with vinegar replaces buttermilk — the acid drives CO2 from baking soda and pushes pH high enough for rapid Maillard browning. The result is tall, tender stacks with a caramelized underside.

title: Fresh egg pasta
desc: Tipo 00 flour and whole eggs, kneaded to silk and rolled thin. The base for tonnarelli, tagliatelle, pappardelle: anywhere a starchy, porous fresh sheet belongs. The gluten structure here is fundamentally different from dried pasta: lower protein, no durum, no extrusion.

title: Thai green curry paste
desc: A pounded-fresh Central Thai green curry paste: green chilies, lemongrass, galangal, makrut lime zest, coriander root, garlic, shallot, shrimp paste. Fresher, oilier, and more aromatic than jarred. The difference is cell-wall rupture: a mortar achieves it and a blade does not.

ingredients.item: green Thai bird's eye chilies, stems removed
green serrano or long green chilies, seeded
lemongrass, white part only, sliced thin
fresh galangal, peeled and sliced thin

title: Acid-milk souring
desc: Add a food acid (vinegar or lemon juice) to whole milk — casein micelles destabilize, viscosity rises, and the liquid is now functionally equivalent to buttermilk for leavening and batter behavior.

title: All-purpose flour
desc: Wheat flour at 10–12% protein. The middle of the wheat-flour band: enough gluten potential for chew, soft enough for tender crumbs when handled gently.

title: Black peppercorns
desc: The unripe drupes of Piper nigrum, dried whole. Heat is piperine (fat-soluble); aroma is a stack of volatile terpenes that vanish within minutes of grinding.

title: Broccoli
desc: Brassica oleracea var. italica. Sulfur-forward when overcooked; nutty and sweet with a hard, dry sear.

title: Butter (unsalted)
desc: Cow's-milk fat with ~16% water and ~2% milk solids. The solids are the flavor lever; the water and the melt curve are why it behaves so differently in different roles.

title: Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋)
desc: Aged glutinous-rice vinegar from Zhenjiang. Dark, malty, smoky, and only mildly acidic; closer in spirit to balsamic than to white vinegar.

title: Dark brown sugar
desc: Granulated sucrose coated with ~6.5% refinery molasses, roughly twice the light-brown ratio. Everything molasses does to a baked good, it does more of here.

title: Dark soy sauce (老抽)
desc: Long-aged Chinese soy sauce finished with molasses or caramel. Used for color and a touch of caramelized depth, not for primary salting.

title: Delicata squash
desc: Cucurbita pepo, Delicata cultivar. Thin edible skin, sweet chestnut-vanilla flesh; the only winter squash you don't have to peel.

title: Eggs (large)
desc: Roughly 50 g per large US egg. Whites set at 62 °C, yolks at 70 °C, and the gap between those temperatures is half of what cooks do with eggs.

title: Evaporated milk
desc: Cow's milk reduced by ~60% with no added sugar, then sterilized in the sealed can. Concentrated dairy body plus the faint Maillard depth that in-can heat builds.

title: Garlic
desc: Allium sativum. Pungency comes from allicin, a sulfur compound that only forms when cells are damaged. How you cut a clove decides how it tastes.

title: Light brown sugar
desc: Granulated sucrose coated with ~3.5% refinery molasses. The molasses adds water, acid, and a toffee-leaning flavor that changes spread, browning, and crumb in everything it touches.

title: Light soy sauce (生抽)
desc: The first pressing of Chinese soy fermentation. Light in color and body, not in salt; carries the bulk of the salinity and glutamates that make soy sauce read as umami.

title: Mirin (hon-mirin)
desc: Sweet rice cooking wine — fermented, alcoholic, glossy. The gloss on teriyaki, the sweetness in dashimaki tamago, the round backnote in nimono.

title: Pecorino romano
desc: Hard sheep's-milk cheese from Lazio and Sardinia, aged 5–8 months and DOP-protected. Salty, sharp, and lactic; functions as a seasoning cheese as much as a flavor cheese.

title: Rice vinegar
desc: Mild, faintly sweet vinegar fermented from rice. ~4–5% acetic acid; gentler than Western vinegars, almost floral on the back end.

title: Sake (cooking)
desc: Fermented rice wine: clean, dry, alcoholic. Tenderizes protein, deglazes pans, and carries volatile aromatics off the heat.

title: Scallion (green onion)
desc: Allium fistulosum or young A. cepa. Mild oniony bite raw, sweet and almost leek-like when seared.

title: Chinese sesame paste (芝麻酱)
desc: Heavily toasted sesame seeds ground to a paste. Darker, deeper, and more bitter than tahini; the toast is the entire reason it exists as a separate ingredient.

title: Shaoxing wine (绍兴酒)
desc: Amber fermented-grain wine from Zhejiang, brewed from glutinous rice and wheat-koji starter and aged in earthenware. The aromatic and amino-acid backbone of Chinese marinades, braises, and stir-fries.

title: Shimeji mushroom
desc: Hypsizygus tessellatus / Hypsizygus marmoreus; small clustered mushroom with a meaty bite and a faint shellfish-sweet note when seared. Bitter raw; transforms above 60°C.

title: Shrimp
desc: Decapod crustacean. Sweet, briny, and proteolytically delicate; overcooks in seconds, locks tough at 65°C+.

title: Sui mi ya cai (碎米芽菜)
desc: Salt-cured, sun-dried, long-fermented and minced stems of a mustard-green cultivar from Sichuan. A dry, dark, glutamate-dense seasoning paste in pickle form.

title: Sweetened condensed milk
desc: Cow's milk reduced by ~60% with ~45% added sugar. The sugar is structural: it shelf-stabilizes the can by osmotic pressure and binds the protein-fat-water emulsion together.

title: Vanilla extract
desc: Cured vanilla pods steeped in ethanol. Vanillin is the headline aromatic; the 250+ supporting compounds are what separates real extract from synthetic.

title: White miso (shiro)
desc: Short-fermented soybean-rice paste, pale beige, mellow and sweet. The miso to default to when a recipe just says "miso."

title: White pepper
desc: The fully ripened Piper nigrum drupe, fermented to break down the outer fruit, then dried as the inner seed alone. Floral, fermented, barnyard. A different spice from black pepper, not a milder version.

title: Whole milk
desc: Cow's milk at ~3.25% fat. Fat carries fat-soluble flavor; casein binds tannins and acid; lactose drives Maillard browning under heat.

title: Wood ear mushroom (木耳)
desc: Dehydrated Auricularia heimuer fungus. Nearly flavorless, structurally chitinous, and bought primarily for the gel-and-crunch texture that survives long cooking.

title: Miso-butter shrimp with delicata, shimeji, and charred broccoli
desc: A one-pan washoku-leaning sheet dish. Delicata roasts to chestnut-sweet edges, broccoli and shimeji char dry, shrimp hit the pan last and the whole thing gets glossed with a miso-mirin-butter sauce finished with rice vinegar.

ingredients.item: shrimp, 21/25 count, peeled and deveined, tails on

title: French
desc: Technique as the transferable unit. The French canon is less about ingredients than about a vocabulary of methods: emulsification, reduction, the roux. These travel across the full range of the cuisine.

title: Indian
desc: Spice as a layered system, not a heat dial. Each spice enters at a specific moment (bloomed in fat, ground dry, added raw at the end) because the volatile compounds respond differently to heat at different stages.

title: Pantry makes
desc: Store-bought replacements that are not actually hard to make and taste meaningfully different. One session sets you up for months.

title: Coconut milk (canned, full-fat)
desc: Pressed coconut emulsion, ~17–22% fat. The lipid phase that carries fat-soluble aromatics in Thai and Southeast Asian curries.

title: Dijon mustard
desc: Brown and yellow mustard seeds soaked in white wine, ground smooth, finished with cider vinegar and salt. Sharp, hot, and creamy in a way no jar ever is.

title: Green curry pork
desc: A coconut-based green curry augmented from store-bought paste with fresh lemongrass, ginger, and shrimp paste. Chinese eggplant absorbs the curry liquor; bamboo and shimeji carry the texture. Two cans of full-fat coconut milk give the dish enough lipid phase to braise without diluting with stock.

ingredients.item: lemongrass, white part only, minced fine
coconut cream, skimmed off the cans
Chinese eggplant, cut in 1-inch oblique chunks
shimeji mushrooms, trimmed into small clusters
pork tenderloin, sliced thin against the grain

title: Homemade panko breadcrumbs
desc: Crustless white bread, dried low and slow, then ground coarse. The result is the long, splintery shard of real panko, not the grainy dust of the supermarket can.

title: Sichuan chili oil
desc: Aromatics infused into hot oil at three precise temperatures, then poured over coarse chili flakes. The crackle is the technique. The result has structure store-bought never does — three layers of red, with sediment that earns its bowl.

ingredients.item: coarse Sichuan or Korean chili flakes (èrjīngtiáo / gochugaru)
fine Sichuan chili flakes or chili powder
neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola)

title: Toasted sesame paste (zhī ma jiàng)
desc: Hulled white sesame seeds toasted dark, ground to a thick caramel-brown paste with a splash of toasted sesame oil. The Chinese pantry version: deeply roasted, structurally distinct from raw Levantine tahini.

title: Whole-grain mustard
desc: Same Dijon chemistry, but pulsed coarse — the seeds stay intact and pop between the teeth, releasing heat in waves rather than all at once.

title: American
desc: Layered fat with restrained sweetness in bakes and bright finishes (lemon, mustard, vinegar) in savory dishes. The throughline is dialing back what factory food overdoes.

title: Chinese
desc: A four-axis pantry (soy, vinegar, aromatic, heat) combined in shifting ratios across regions. Every dish reads the cook's hand on those four levers.

title: Italian
desc: Restraint plus fat: olive oil, cured pork, aged cheese, acid. The technique is in the ratios, not the ingredient count.

title: Thai
desc: Balance across four registers (sweet, salt, heat, sour) held within a single dish, not separated across courses. The Thai catalog here is drinks, for now, and they teach extraction.

title: Digital Scale
desc: Weighs ingredients in grams. The single most consequential tool for repeatable baking and accurate recipe scaling.

title: Dutch Oven
desc: 5–7 quart enameled cast iron pot with a heavy lid. Mass for steady braising, seal for moisture, depth for stocks — and stovetop-to-oven without a transfer.

title: Heavy Saucepan
desc: 2–3 quart pan with a thick conductive base. Thermal mass is the feature: stable low heat for tea, custards, and reductions that won't scorch.

title: Instant-Read Thermometer
desc: Probe thermometer that reads internal temperature in 1–3 seconds. Replaces guesswork with a number on protein doneness, custards, brown butter, and oil.

title: Microplane
desc: Photo-etched fine grater. Turns hard cheese, citrus zest, garlic, and ginger into a fluffy, dissolvable powder.

title: Wide Skillet
desc: 12-inch sauté pan with sloped sides. The width is the spec: surface area drives evaporation, browning, and the room you need to finish pasta in the pan.

title: Wok
desc: High-walled carbon steel pan for stir-frying. The shape concentrates heat at the base and routes ingredients up the cooler walls between bursts of contact.

title: Cook
desc: Tested recipes. Filter by cuisine, course, diet, time, and difficulty.

title: Explore
desc: Four families gather every entry. Pick a family for a focused walk, or jump straight to a category.

title: Learn
desc: Techniques that show up across many recipes — what they are, when they matter, how they fail.

title: Pantry
desc: What ingredients are, how to choose and store them, what to swap. Plus the equipment that earns its drawer.

title: Traverse
desc: Cross-cutting indices into the library — by region, by curated path, by attribute.

title: Chinese staples
desc: The dishes that teach the Chinese pantry: the light/dark soy distinction, Chinkiang acid, Sichuan má-là, and sesame-paste body.

title: Holiday table
desc: A curated meal — protein, sides, dessert — that holds together as one table rather than four good dishes that happen to share a date.

title: Tea drinks
desc: Extraction is the technique. Each of these gets the leaf right before it gets anything else right.

title: Weekend bake
desc: Bakes that reward weekend time: brown butter, properly creamed butter and sugar, cocoa bloomed before it hits the batter.

title: Weeknight pasta
desc: Pasta and noodle dishes that hit the table fast without sacrificing technique. Start with the fresh dough to understand what the sauces are working with.

title: Assam tea
desc: Robust, malty black tea from northeast India. The default base for masala chai and what gives English Breakfast its backbone.

title: Avocado oil
desc: Pressed from avocado flesh, mostly monounsaturated fat. Holds high heat without smoking and stays neutral enough for searing where olive oil's fruit notes would intrude.

title: Baking powder
desc: A pre-mixed chemical leavener: baking soda plus one or two dry acids plus a starch buffer. Double-acting versions release CO2 in two stages — once when wet, again at oven heat.

title: Baking soda
desc: Pure sodium bicarbonate. An alkali that needs an acid in the batter to release CO2 and lift — and that simultaneously raises pH, deepening browning.

title: Chili oil (辣椒油)
desc: Neutral oil infused with dried chilies and aromatics, with the toasted chili sediment retained. Both the clear oil and the sediment carry distinct functions in a dish.

title: Cinnamon stick
desc: Dried inner bark of Cinnamomum trees. Two main types: hot, sharp Cassia (the supermarket default) and floral, gentler Ceylon ("true" cinnamon).

title: Dark chocolate
desc: Cocoa solids and cocoa butter combined with sugar, with no milk solids. The cacao percentage on the label is solids + butter together, not solids alone.

title: Dried osmanthus (桂花)
desc: Dried flowers of Osmanthus fragrans — tiny golden florets used as a floral garnish and tea-blender. Carries a stone-fruit and apricot character from γ-decalactone.

title: Dutch-process cocoa
desc: Cocoa powder treated with potassium carbonate to neutralize its natural acidity. Darker, mellower, and chemically incompatible with recipes built around natural cocoa.

title: Fine sea salt
desc: Evaporated seawater, ground fine. Small crystals dissolve fast and distribute evenly — the right salt for baking and for anything that needs to disappear.

title: Ginger (fresh)
desc: Rhizome of Zingiber officinale. Sharp, peppery heat from gingerol when raw; mellower and sweeter (zingerone) when cooked.

title: Granulated sugar
desc: Refined sucrose, medium crystal. Does four jobs in baking — aerates, structures, browns, and holds water — and it's the default for a reason.

title: Heavy cream
desc: Cow's cream at ≥36% milkfat. The fat content is what lets it whip and what keeps it stable when reduced.

title: Kosher salt
desc: Coarse-grain sodium chloride, no iodine, no anti-caking agents. Cooking salt of choice because the crystal shape distributes evenly by hand.

title: Rock sugar (冰糖)
desc: Recrystallized sucrose in irregular amber or clear chunks. Dissolves slowly and at lower osmotic shock than granulated sugar, which is why Chinese braises use it for the finishing gloss.

title: Sichuan peppercorns
desc: The husks of Zanthoxylum berries. Not actually pepper — they create má, the buzzing tingling numbness that defines Sichuan cooking.

title: Star anise
desc: The dried fruit of Illicium verum, eight-pointed and intensely licorice-aromatic. The soul of Chinese five-spice and the unmistakable note in Thai iced tea.

title: Toasted sesame oil
desc: Oil pressed from roasted sesame seeds. A finishing ingredient — its aromatic compounds are heat-volatile and degrade fast above 175°C.

title: Vanilla bean paste
desc: Vanilla extract suspended in a syrup with the scraped seeds of the pod. Same flavor as a whole bean, a fraction of the price, and you get the visible black flecks.

title: Brown butter cacio e pepe
desc: Roman three-ingredient pasta, weaponized: brown butter replaces olive oil to layer Maillard nuttiness over the pecorino lactic-tang and bloomed peppercorn piperine.

title: Brown butter sugar cookies
desc: Sugar cookies built on melted brown butter — chewy and dense in the center, with a crackled sugar shell on top.

title: Chai pumpkin cupcakes
desc: Pumpkin cupcakes with the spice infused into the milk like a real chai, then frosted with brown butter cream cheese.

ingredients.item: avocado oil or light olive oil

title: Chinese tea eggs
desc: Soft-boiled eggs cracked all over and steeped in spiced soy and black tea. The cracks become a marble pattern in the white.

title: Cocoa-osmanthus dun nai
desc: Steamed milk pudding infused with cocoa nibs and finished with osmanthus and oolong — bitter, floral, silken.

title: Double dark chocolate crinkle cookies
desc: Fudgy crinkle cookies built on melted dark chocolate, bloomed cocoa, and a coconut-oil tweak that keeps them glossy and chewy.

title: Vanilla dun nai (steamed milk custard)
desc: Cantonese steamed milk custard with vanilla bean — silken, barely set, and finished in a Dutch oven using residual heat to avoid honeycomb.

title: House blend chai
desc: A small-batch masala chai built on a freshly ground whole-spice blend. Two cups, fifteen minutes, no mix.

title: Infused mashed potatoes
desc: Yukon Golds boiled in their skins, riced hot, and folded with milk and butter that's been steeped with garlic, thyme, rosemary, and bay.

title: Lemony green beans with garlic crunch
desc: Blanched green beans tossed with butter and lemon, finished with a garlicky panko crumb that stays crisp on top.

title: Maple and mustard glazed turkey breast
desc: Bone-in turkey breast dry-brined overnight, roasted with herb butter under the skin, and lacquered with a maple-mustard glaze at the end.

title: Midnight velvet mini cake with strawberry and balsamic caviar
desc: A two-layer 4-inch chocolate cake bloomed with hot coffee, filled with strawberry reduction, finished with strawberry meringue buttercream, balsamic pearls, and tempered chocolate shards.

title: Sichuan dandan mian
desc: Wheat noodles in a sesame-chili sauce, topped with crispy rendered pork and ya cai. The sauce comes together in a bowl while the noodles boil.

ingredients.item: sui mi ya cai (preserved mustard greens)

title: Smashed cucumber and wood ear salad
desc: Crushed Persian cucumbers and rehydrated wood ear in a Chinkiang vinegar dressing — sharp, snappy, the right counter to anything heavy.

ingredients.item: wood ear mushrooms, rehydrated and trimmed

title: Thai iced tea (no dye)
desc: Small-batch Thai iced tea built on whole spices and loose-leaf black tea — no orange food coloring, low astringency, evaporated milk float.

ingredients.item: loose-leaf black tea (Assam, Ceylon, or English Breakfast)

title: Thai iced tea with salted vanilla whip
desc: Spiced black tea sweetened with brown sugar and condensed milk, finished with a soft-peak salted vanilla cream that floats and slowly cascades.

title: Blooming aromatics
desc: Heat whole spices in fat to release lipid-soluble volatiles that water cannot extract.

title: Blooming cocoa
desc: Pour hot liquid or melted fat over cocoa powder and rest before incorporating — heat plus moisture extracts aromatics that stay locked in dry powder.

title: Brown butter (beurre noisette)
desc: Cook butter past the foaming stage until milk solids turn amber — yields hazelnut aroma and Maillard depth from the casein toasting.

title: Creaming butter and sugar
desc: Shear sugar crystals into pliable butter to drag air into the fat phase — the bubbles leaven cookies and cakes alongside chemical leaveners.

title: Deglazing
desc: Add liquid to a hot pan after a sear so the fond — browned protein and sugar residue — dissolves into it and becomes the body of a sauce.

title: Dry brining
desc: Salt a protein surface and rest it long enough for osmosis to reverse — water and salt diffuse back into the meat, seasoning it throughout.

title: Garlic crunch
desc: Shallow-fry sliced garlic in oil to amber and pull off-heat — crisp texture, sweet Maillard, no bitterness.

title: Ice bath shock
desc: Plunge blanched vegetables into ice water to arrest carry-over cooking and lock chlorophyll bright before it degrades to gray-green pheophytin.

title: Maillard sear
desc: Drive amino acids and reducing sugars on a dry protein surface past 140C to build a browned crust and several hundred new flavor compounds.

title: Pan emulsion (mantecare)
desc: Bind starchy pasta water and fat into a glossy sauce by tossing pasta with fat over low heat, finishing off-heat with cheese.

title: Smacking vegetables
desc: Crush firm vegetables with the side of a knife or cleaver to rupture cell walls — more sauce uptake, more flavor, jagged surfaces.

title: Steeping tea
desc: Extract polyphenols, theanine, and tannins from tea leaves on a time-and-temperature curve that the leaf grade dictates.

title: Tempering eggs
desc: Equalize egg temperature with a hot liquid in stages so the proteins set smoothly instead of curdling.

title: Bao cui (薄脆, thin fried wheat cracker for jianbing)
desc: The thin, shatter-crisp fried wheat cracker that is the canonical filling for Tianjin jianbing guozi. A lye-or-baking-soda-leavened, rolled-paper-thin wheat dough cut into 18 × 12 cm rectangles, shallow-fried in 180°C oil for 60 to 90 seconds per side until pale gold and glass-shatter. Stub.

title: Homemade tianmianjiang (sweet bean sauce, 甜面酱, 6-week home cure)
desc: A home cure of fermented wheat flour and soybean to produce the sweet-savory dark-brown paste that is the foundational sauce of northern-Chinese cooking. A 6-to-8 week project using a koji-cultured wheat dough as the enzymatic engine and a salt cure to develop depth. Not authentic 6-month commercial tianmianjiang but the closest reasonable home version. Stub.

ingredients.item: soybeans, soaked overnight and steamed until tender

title: Jianbing chili paste (the brushable heat axis for Tianjin jianbing)
desc: The street-vendor chili condiment for jianbing: a thick brushable paste of chili oil, fresh-pounded garlic, doubanjiang (fermented broad bean), Sichuan peppercorn, and a finish of toasted sesame oil. Distinct from bottled chili oil because the fresh garlic and bean paste give it dimension; distinct from doubanjiang alone because the chili oil base makes it brushable. Stub.

ingredients.item: doubanjiang (Pixian-style fermented broad bean and chili paste)
Sichuan peppercorns, lightly toasted and ground

title: Ube halaya
desc: Filipino jam of grated purple yam (Dioscorea alata) slow-cooked with coconut milk, sweetened condensed milk, evaporated milk, and butter until thick, glossy, and spreadable. Stub.

ingredients.item: grated ube (frozen, thawed and drained; or fresh boiled and riced)

title: Black beans from dried
desc: Dried black beans soaked overnight then simmered with onion, bay, and a strip of kombu into a creamy bean and a richly seasoned bean liquor. The structural pot of beans for Mexican, Cuban, and African-American cooking. Stub.

title: Chaat masala
desc: Toasted-and-ground North Indian finishing spice blend ; kala namak, amchur, cumin, coriander, ajwain, ginger, chili. Sulfur-sour-saline axis. Stub.

title: Chipotle in adobo (homemade)
desc: Dried chipotle morita peppers rehydrated in a tomato-vinegar-aromatic sauce. The fresher home version of the canned product, with markedly less of the metallic tinned-tomato note. Stub.

ingredients.item: avocado oil or other neutral high-heat oil

title: Strained Greek yogurt
desc: Whole-milk yogurt cultured at 42 to 45 °C, then strained 4 to 8 hours through cheesecloth to concentrate the protein and remove the whey. The cooked-in-cheesecloth-bag method versus the stand-mixer-bowl-and-sieve method. Stub.

ingredients.item: whole milk (full-fat, 3.5 to 4 percent butterfat; non-ultra-pasteurized for best texture)
plain whole-milk yogurt with live active cultures (as starter)

title: Homemade bacon
desc: Pork belly cured for 7 days in salt, pink curing salt #1 (sodium nitrite), sugar, and aromatics, then cold-smoked over hardwood for 12 to 24 hours. The home-cured product is markedly more complex than supermarket bacon, with the cure profile tuned to the cook's preference and the smoke level chosen by the cook. Weekend project; 10 days elapsed time, 4 hours active. Stub.

ingredients.item: pink curing salt #1 (sodium nitrite 6.25 percent, salt 93.75 percent)
hardwood for smoking (hickory, apple, maple, or cherry)

title: Homemade tomato paste (slow-roasted concentrate)
desc: Whole canned tomatoes reduced over 6 to 8 hours of low-oven roasting into a thick, deep-rust-red concentrate; the homemade tomato paste that distinguishes from the metallic can. The structural umami amplifier for cioppino, pasta sauces, and braises, with a layered flavor profile that includes caramelized fruit sugars (from Maillard browning) and reduced glutamate concentration (from water removal). A weekend project; one batch fills a small jar that lasts months in the refrigerator under a layer of olive oil.

ingredients.item: canned San Marzano whole peeled tomatoes (or Bianco DiNapoli, Pomi, any good-quality)

title: Infused honey (thyme, rosemary, chili, lavender)
desc: Aromatic honey made by submerging fresh herbs, dried chilies, or other aromatics in raw honey at 50 °C for 2 to 4 hours. The honey takes on the herb aromatic without driving off its native floral character. Stub.

ingredients.item: raw honey (acacia, wildflower, or thyme — match to the infusion)
fresh thyme sprigs (or rosemary, or 2 dried chilies, or 1 tbsp dried lavender)

title: Light soy sauce (生抽)
desc: Soybeans and wheat fermented with Aspergillus mold for 6 to 18 months, pressed and pasteurized into the lighter, saltier seasoning soy of Chinese cooking. Stub.

ingredients.item: whole wheat berries, lightly toasted and cracked

title: Red wine vinaigrette
desc: A canonical Mediterranean salad dressing: 1:3 red wine vinegar to extra-virgin olive oil, dijon as the emulsifier, mashed garlic and salt dissolved in the acid phase before the oil streams in. Holds 30 to 60 minutes after building. Stub.

title: Seafood stock (shellfish and fin-fish bone broth)
desc: A clean, aromatic seafood stock built from shrimp shells, white-fish bones (cod, halibut, snapper carcasses), crab shells, and a Western mirepoix (onion, fennel, leek, garlic). Roasted-shell version for cioppino and bouillabaisse depth; raw-shell version for delicate fumets and risotto al frutti di mare. The stock cooks fast (45 minutes) compared to meat broths because fish bones release their flavor and gelatin quickly, and over-simmering produces a bitter, fishy-aggressive result.

ingredients.item: shrimp shells (saved from prep, frozen until needed)
fish bones and head (cod, halibut, snapper, branzino), gills removed and rinsed
crab shells (cleaned, picked, broken into pieces)
leek, white and pale green only, sliced

title: Shaoxing wine (绍兴酒)
desc: Glutinous rice and wheat fermented with qu starter for 6 to 12 months, producing the amber rice wine that anchors most Chinese cooking. Stub.

ingredients.item: qu starter (Chinese rice-wine starter; jiu qu)

title: Thai sticky rice (khao niao)
desc: Glutinous rice soaked, then steamed in a bamboo basket over simmering water until each grain is translucent, chewy, and pinch-able. The table rice of Laos and northeast Thailand; the eating implement for larb, nam tok, and som tum. Stub.

title: Worcestershire sauce
desc: A long-fermented English condiment of malt vinegar, anchovy, tamarind, molasses, garlic, onion, and warm spices, aged 6 to 18 months in the bottle. Stub.

title: Country sourdough
desc: A simple white-flour country sourdough loaf: 1000 g flour, 720 g water, 200 g levain, 22 g salt. Bulk ferment 4 hours at 22-24°C with stretch-and-folds, shape, cold-proof overnight, bake in a preheated Dutch oven. Stub.

title: Fresh potato gnocchi
desc: Hand-rolled potato dumplings: baked russets riced fine, bound with the minimum egg and flour the dough will tolerate, formed into pillows, and cooked just past floating. The technique is built around moisture management; everything else follows. Stub.

title: Fresh soy milk (homemade, from soaked soybeans)
desc: Soaked soybeans blended with water, cooked at low simmer, strained through cheesecloth — the foundational base for fresh douhua, hot-set silken tofu, and any application where the soy milk itself is the dominant flavor. Stub.

title: Ginger-garlic paste (homemade adrak-lehsun)
desc: Fresh ginger and garlic blended in roughly equal parts with a small amount of oil and salt to a smooth paste. The structural aromatic base of nearly every South Asian curry, marinade, and stir-fry. Five minutes of work, lasts a week refrigerated. Stub.

title: Coconut milk (homemade from grated mature coconut)
desc: Mature grated coconut blended with hot water and squeezed through cheesecloth, twice. The first pressing yields thick "coconut cream" used for finishing curries; the second pressing yields thinner "coconut milk" used as a base. Stub.

ingredients.item: mature coconut, grated (fresh or frozen unsweetened)

title: Homemade doubanjiang (short-cure version)
desc: A 4-6 week home version of Pixian-style chili bean paste, using fermented broad beans and dried chilies. Stub. The authentic 1-3 year cure is not practical at home; this is the closest short-cycle approximation.

ingredients.item: fresh red erjingtiao chilies (or Mexican guajillo + chiles de árbol blend)

title: Fish sauce (homemade Vietnamese-style)
desc: Anchovies layered with sea salt at a 1:3 ratio, sealed in a glass or ceramic vat, and left to ferment in a warm dark place for 12 to 18 months. The endogenous fish enzymes hydrolyze the proteins into the amber liquid that defines Southeast Asian cooking. Stub — a months-long project, not a weekend recipe.

ingredients.item: whole fresh anchovies (or other small oily fish — sardines, herring, smelt)
unrefined sea salt (no iodine, no anti-caking agents)

title: Garam masala (homemade fresh-toasted blend)
desc: Whole spices toasted in a dry pan until fragrant, cooled, and ground to a fine powder. Dramatically more aromatic than supermarket pre-ground; lasts 6 to 8 weeks at peak. The North Indian / Pakistani household standard. Stub.

title: Gochujang (homemade Korean fermented chili paste)
desc: Glutinous rice cooked into porridge, blended with meju powder, gochugaru, and salt, fermented in earthen jars or sealed containers for 6 months minimum (ideally 1 to 3 years) until the rice starches convert to sugars and the flavor deepens into the signature sweet-spicy-funky-savory paste. Stub — a multi-month commitment.

title: Homemade mayonnaise
desc: Egg yolk, neutral oil, lemon juice or vinegar, mustard, and salt, emulsified by hand or with an immersion blender into a thick, glossy, intensely-flavored mayo that reads dramatically different from the commercial jar. Stub.

ingredients.item: large egg yolk (or whole egg for the immersion-blender route)
fresh lemon juice or white wine vinegar
neutral oil (avocado, grapeseed, or refined sunflower)

title: Oyster sauce (homemade)
desc: Fresh oysters simmered with their liquor, soy sauce, sugar, and aromatics, then strained and reduced into a glossy dark sauce. The Cantonese flavor base built from scratch — fresher and significantly more complex than commercial bottles. Stub.

ingredients.item: fresh shucked oysters with their liquor

title: Puff pastry (homemade rough puff)
desc: Flour and cold butter laminated through 4 to 6 fold-and-rest cycles into a flaky, layered dough. Quicker than classic French puff pastry (which uses a butter block) but produces 95% of the rise and flake. A weekend-baking commitment. Stub.

title: Okaka (soy-and-sesame bonito flake mix)
desc: Katsuobushi tossed with soy sauce, mirin, toasted sesame oil, and a small pinch of sugar; left to marinate 10 minutes until the flakes absorb the seasoning into a damp-savory clump. The two-minute onigiri filling, rice topping, and pantry staple. Stub.

title: Shiozake (salt-cured broiled salmon)
desc: Salmon fillet dry-cured in coarse salt for 24 hours to firm the flesh and concentrate flavor, then broiled until the skin is crisp and the interior just opaque. The canonical onigiri filling, breakfast fish, and bento staple. Stub.

ingredients.item: skin-on salmon fillet (sockeye, king, or coho)
coarse sea salt (or kosher salt, Diamond Crystal)

title: Sweetened condensed milk
desc: Whole milk reduced with sugar to a viscous, shelf-stable syrup. Three hours of slow reduction yields a fresher, less metallic alternative to canned. Stub.

title: Umeboshi (salt-pickled and sun-dried plums)
desc: Sour-salty preserved plums made by lacto-fermenting unripe Japanese ume in coarse salt for several weeks, then sun-drying for three consecutive hot days, optionally re-pickling with red shiso for the canonical pink color. The umami-and-acid hammer of Japanese pickles. Stub.

ingredients.item: unripe ume plums (yellow-blushing, just before full ripeness)
fresh red shiso leaves (akajiso), washed and dried
shōchū or vodka, for sterilizing the crock

title: Youtiao (Chinese fried dough sticks)
desc: The canonical Chinese breakfast bread: a yeasted-and-leavened wheat dough cut into long strips, paired in twos, and deep-fried into hollow shatteringly-crisp golden batons. Eaten torn into hot congee or dipped in warm soy milk. Stub.

ingredients.item: all-purpose flour (or bread flour for chewier crumb)
neutral oil for deep-frying (peanut, rice bran, or canola)

title: Shrak (Bedouin paper-thin flatbread)
desc: Hand-stretched, paper-thin whole-wheat flatbread cooked in seconds on a domed saj. The bread layer beneath mansaf, and a Bedouin staple in its own right. Stub.
